content:
You are a creative and professional JavaScript game developer who builds polished and visually appealing video games using p5.js primitives.
Your game should offer a fun and engaging game experience for the player following game development principles.
Create a learnable progression ramp that balances learning, fun, and challenge.
Use your creativity to enrich the concept into an innovative design before coding, adding interesting and consistent elements beyond the game concept.
Ensure the implementation is free of inconsistencies and errors. Write modular ES6 code with automated tests covering key aspects of the game.
Follow the hard constraints exactly; violating any is a critical error.

<hard_constraints>
- The full game can be expanded by adding more elements to the game concept but should align with the game concept respecting the hard constraints and game design principles.
- Game must load and function without errors, start on pressing ENTER, gameplay must be responsive to player inputs, and have correct game phase handling.
- Game phases: START→PLAYING (ENTER), PLAYING→GAME_OVER (win/lose), GAME_OVER→START (R), PLAYING↔PAUSED (ESC). Expose this in a variable called `gameState.gamePhase` where `gameState` is a global object accessible via `window.getGameState()`.
- Start screen should have a title, short description of the game, instructions to play the game, and a "PRESS ENTER TO START" prompt.
- Allowed libraries: p5.js, p5.collide2D
- Canvas: `600 × 400 px`; target 60 FPS
- Keyboard inputs only. No mouse control.
- Allowed gameplay control keys: Arrow keys (37-40), Space (32), Shift (16), Z (90)
- Game phase specific controls:
    - ENTER (13)  – start the game
    - ESC (27)    – pause / unpause
    - R (82)      – restart; returns to the start screen, where the player can press ENTER to play again
- No external images, sprites, fonts, or other assets. Make visual assets and animations using code.
- No audio or sound effects.
- Use p5.js in instance mode and expose the game instance globally as window.gameInstance.
- Ensure game reproducibility using `p.randomSeed(42)` in the `setup` function. No other random seeding.
- Use ES6 syntax with proper imports and exports at the top of the file where they are used. No dynamic imports or require() imports.
- Maintain `p.logs` as write-only; log game_info, inputs, player_info with proper initialization and updates during the game loop.
</hard_constraints>

<instructions>
<game_concept_to_game_design>
Expand the game concept with new game elements with a well-planned progression and learning curve that enables the player to learn about the game elements and mechanics.
For inspiration, following are some examples of game elements that you can add to the game design as long as the final game satisfies the requirements of the game concept:
- environment: game setting, genre, viewpoint
- entities: characters, opponents, collaborators, NPCs, objects in the game, power-ups, destructible objects, obstacles
- mechanics: rules of the games, final win conditions, game over conditions, subgoals, rewards, player actions and abilities, interaction between entities
- graphics: visual appearance and animations of the game entities and the environment
</game_concept_to_game_design>

<code_instructions>
You are encouraged to write as much code as you can to make a fun game, aesthetically appealing, and provide a fun gameplay experience for the player when playing the game for the first time.

<code_organization>
- Modular code organization with proper ES6 imports:
  - Organize the code into multiple files as needed (game.js, globals.js, etc.)
  - Group related functionality together in meaningful modules
  - Proper imports:
    - Use ES6 for imports and exports
    - Every file MUST import ALL constants, variables, functions, classes, and external symbols at the top of every file. No Node.js style require imports. No dynamic imports within functions
```javascript
// Essential pattern (ALWAYS follow this)
import { Player, Enemy } from './entities.js';
import { checkCollision, applyPhysics } from './physics.js';
import { gameState, CANVAS_WIDTH } from './globals.js';

// Example function with proper dependencies
function createPlayer(x, y) {
  const player = new Player(x, y);
  gameState.player = player;
  gameState.entities.push(player);
  return player;
}
```

- State Management:
  - Implement `function getGameState()` and attach it to `window`. It must return the `gameState` object.
  - Use a single `gameState` object to track the game's current data and status. Store all game data in the `gameState` object to be used by automated testing code to play-test the game.    
```javascript
export const gameState = {
  player: null,       // player entity to be initialized in the setup function and initialized before the game starts
  entities: [],       // all game entities including player
  score: 0,
  gamePhase: "START", // "START", "PLAYING", "GAME_OVER_WIN/LOSE", "PAUSED"
  controlMode: "HUMAN", // "HUMAN", "TEST_1", "TEST_2", "TEST_3", etc.
  ... // other game state variables which are needed to be tracked for automated testing
};
```
</code_organization>

<p5_instructions>
- Use p5.js in instance mode and store the p5 instance in a variable called `gameInstance`. Expose the game instance globally as follows:
```javascript
const p5 = window.p5
let gameInstance = new p5(p => {
    // Initialize variables
    ...
    // Initialize the logs. Important: Do not reset the logs at any point in the code! logs are considered write-only!
    p.logs = {
        "game_info": [],  // Information about the game
        "inputs": [],     // Information about the key inputs
        "player_info": [] // Information about the player character
    };

    // Functions
    ...
});
// Expose the game instance globally
window.gameInstance = gameInstance;
```
- p.logs is write-only. Do not reset it. We want to keep track of all the logs since the start of the p5.js game instance.
- Variables to store in the p.logs during gameplay:
  - In "game_info": Information about the game when there are changes in the game phases
    - "data": Any data like game phase or anything else you want to log
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event accessed using `p.frameCount` and `Date.now()` respectively
  - In "inputs": Store the control inputs when they are triggered by the player
    - "input_type": The input event type (keyPressed, keyReleased, etc.)
    - "data": Additional data specific to the input type. Store `key` and `keyCode` if the input is a key.
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event
  - In "player_info": Information about the player character when there are changes in the player's state (accessed via `gameState.player` where `gameState` is accessible via `window.getGameState()`)
    - "screen_x", "screen_y": The x and y position of the player on the screen
    - "game_x", "game_y": The x and y position of the player in the game world
    - "framecount": The framecount of the event

- Useful Functions:
  - Use p. to call p5 functions.
  - p5: `keyIsDown(key_code)`, `noLoop()/loop()`, `tint()`, `push()/pop()`, `textAlign()`, `textSize()`, `text()`
  - p5.collide2d for collision detection
    - Note that the specific order of the shapes in the function name matters. For example, 'collideCircleRect' is not available.
    - Available functions: `collidePointPoint`, `collidePointCircle`, `collidePointEllipse`, `collidePointRect`, `collidePointLine`, `collidePointArc`, `collideRectRect`, `collideCircleCircle`, `collideRectCircle`, `collideLineLine`, `collideLineCircle`, `collideLineRect`, `collidePointPoly`, `collideCirclePoly`, `collideRectPoly`, `collideLinePoly`, `collidePolyPoly`, `collidePointTriangle`
</p5_instructions>

<game_mechanics_instructions>
Implement intuitive, believable mechanics that enable players to achieve the final goal.
Choose parameters and logic for game mechanics that are intuitive and believable. These can change as the game progresses. 
Even at the end of the game, the mechanics must be such that the player can win by showing their learned skill.
- Controls:
  - Player controls must be intuitive and impact the game state in a meaningful way
  - Input handling: Use keyboard for HUMAN mode, automated testing action outputs for automated testing modes
  - When automated testing mode is selected, the game must automatically play using the action outputs from the automated testing controller
- Non-player characters and objects:
  - NPCs and objects must be spawned and behave respecting the progression of the game
  - Implement intelligent, intuitive, and challenging behavior for all game entities to keep the player engaged
- Interactions with other entities and objects:
  - Have plausible physics with parameters and logic to give it a believable realistic feel
  - Prevent objects from passing through each other or behaving inconsistently to prevent shocking the player
- GAME_OVER Conditions:
  - Implement mechanics that can lead to win or lose conditions achievable by the player
  - GAME_OVER must be a final state followed by game over screen
- Rewards:
  - Define meaningful and reasonable conditions for rewards to keep the player motivated to reach the final goal
  - Do not excessively repeat the same rewards
</game_mechanics_instructions>

<rendering_instructions>
- Implement aesthetic objects, smooth animations, and dynamic effects for the game environment, entities, and their interactions
- Provide useful visual feedback:
  - Player character must be distinctly identifiable
  - Provide visual feedback for interactions and response to their actions
  - UI: Add well-positioned text and graphics to inform the player about information like score, health, etc.
  - Never display current controlMode (HUMAN/TESTING) on the game canvas.
- Ensure the correct render order of the game objects to ensure player is visible on the screen
- Avoid flickering:
  - Do not call p.randomSeed() inside draw()
  - Have exactly one call to p.background() at the top of draw()
  - Do not draw elements that are randomly sampled at every frame as this causes flickering
  - Use ternary operator to set colors for conditional rendering: Example: `p.fill(...(CONDITION ? [255, 220, 150] : [40, 30, 20]));`
- Required Informative Game Screens:
  - Start: Title, clear instructions, objectives, and controls with actions for the player, "PRESS ENTER TO START"
  - Playing: Game rendering, score, and active gameplay
  - Pause: Small text "PAUSED" indicator in top right
  - Game Over: Win/Lose message, final score, "PRESS R TO RESTART", R takes you back to the start screen
</rendering_instructions>

</code_instructions>
</instructions>


<task>
Implement a complete, fun, and error-free p5.js game for the following concept:
{
  "game_name": "Subway Surfers",
  "genre": "Action",
  "core_mechanics": "The game centers on a character automatically moving forward on three parallel tracks. Player actions modify the character's position and state:\n-   **Lane Change:** Triggered by swiping left/right. The character's horizontal (X) position smoothly interpolates to the center of the target lane. This involves updating `player.targetLaneX` and animating `player.x` over a short duration.\n-   **Jump:** Triggered by swiping up. `player.isJumping` becomes true. The character's vertical (Y) position increases to a peak height and then decreases due to simulated gravity. Player's bounding box height is adjusted during the jump arc. `player.isJumping` becomes false upon landing.\n-   **Slide:** Triggered by swiping down. `player.isSliding` becomes true. The character's vertical (Y) position lowers, and its bounding box height is significantly reduced for a fixed duration. `player.isSliding` becomes false after the duration.\n-   **Automatic Forward Movement:** The game world (obstacles, coins, power-ups) continuously scrolls towards the player along the Z-axis. The `gameSpeed` variable dictates this scrolling rate, increasing over time within a level and at level transitions.\n-   **Obstacle Interaction:** \n    -   Collision Detection: Player's bounding box (adjusted for jump/slide state) is checked against obstacle bounding boxes.\n    -   If a collision occurs: If `player.hoverboardActive` is true, the hoverboard is consumed (`player.hoverboardActive = false`), and gameplay continues. Otherwise, the game transitions to `GAME_OVER`.\n-   **Coin Collection:** If `player.hoverboardActive` is true, or if `player.magnetActive` is true, or if the player's bounding box intersects a coin's bounding box, the coin is collected. Collected coins increment `coinsCollected` and `score`.\n-   **Power-up Activation:** When a player collects a power-up, its effect is activated for a set duration (`powerupTimer`):\n    -   **Coin Magnet:** All coins in all three lanes are automatically collected within a certain radius of the player.\n    -   **Hoverboard:** Grants one-time collision protection.\n    -   **Jetpack:** Player automatically flies above all obstacles and collects all coins for a duration.\n-   **State Tracking:** Key states to track include: `player.x`, `player.y`, `player.isJumping`, `player.isSliding`, `player.currentLaneIndex`, `player.hoverboardActive`, `player.jetpackActive`, `player.magnetActive`, `gameSpeed`, `distanceRun`, `coinsCollected`, `score`, `currentLevel`, `gameState` (MENU, PLAYING, PAUSED, LEVEL_COMPLETE, GAME_OVER, WIN), and for each spawned object: `object.x`, `object.y`, `object.z`, `object.type`.\n-   **Procedural Generation:** Obstacles, coins, and power-ups are spawned ahead of the player at varying `z` distances based on current level and difficulty parameters. Objects are recycled using object pooling.",
  "visual_elements": "The game will render a pseudo-3D environment using 2D p5.js drawing primitives (rect, circle, triangle) or simple sprites.\n-   **Player Character:** Represented as a blue rectangle (e.g., 20x40 pixels). Animations: running (simple bobbing), jumping (elongating/shortening, moving up/down), sliding (squashing, moving down), flying (rocket-like shape or player with jetpack overlay). Visual effect for hoverboard: player outline glows orange. Visual effect for jetpack: player has a rocket-like sprite attached, flames at bottom.\n-   **Obstacles:**\n    -   **Trains:** Large grey/red rectangles (e.g., 80x100 pixels when at player's Z-depth), varying in width to cover 1, 2, or 3 lanes. Appears larger as it approaches.\n    -   **Jump Barriers:** Low yellow/black striped rectangles (e.g., 60x20 pixels), spanning one or more lanes.\n    -   **Slide Barriers:** High red horizontal beams (e.g., 60x15 pixels), positioned above the player's normal running height.\n-   **Coins:** Small yellow circles (e.g., 10px diameter), possibly with a white sparkle effect, positioned at various `x` and `y` coordinates on each lane.\n-   **Power-ups:** Represented by distinct icons:\n    -   **Jetpack:** Green rocket-shaped icon.\n    -   **Hoverboard:** Orange board-shaped icon.\n    -   **Coin Magnet:** Purple magnet-shaped icon.\n-   **Tracks/Ground:** Three parallel grey lines or rectangles on a darker ground plane, scrolling towards the player. Perspective effect: lines converge towards the horizon.\n-   **Background:** Simple distant city/subway tunnel elements (e.g., abstract shapes or very simplified buildings) that scroll slower than the foreground to create depth.\n-   **UI Elements (p5.js text and shapes):\n    -   **Score Display:** Top-left corner (e.g., `text('SCORE: ' + score, 10, 30)`).\n    -   **Distance Display:** Top-right corner (e.g., `text('DIST: ' + distanceRun + 'm', width - 150, 30)`).\n    -   **Coin Count:** Below Score (e.g., `text('COINS: ' + coinsCollected, 10, 50)`).\n    -   **Level Indicator:** Top-center (e.g., `text('LEVEL: ' + currentLevel, width / 2, 30)`).\n    -   **Power-up Icons/Timers:** Small icons (jetpack, hoverboard, magnet) along the top of the screen, with a countdown bar/number indicating remaining duration.\n    -   **Game State Screens:** Centered text for 'Game Over', 'Level Complete', 'Paused', 'Win' along with relevant scores and interactive buttons (rectangles with text) like 'Restart', 'Next Level', 'Resume', 'Quit to Menu'.",
  "control_scheme": "Keyboard controls for player actions:\n-   `ArrowLeft` or `A`: Change lane to the left.\n-   `ArrowRight` or `D`: Change lane to the right.\n-   `ArrowUp` or `W`: Jump.\n-   `ArrowDown` or `S`: Slide.\n-   `Space`: \n    -   In `MENU` state: Start game.\n    -   In `PLAYING` state: Pause/Unpause game.\n    -   In `LEVEL_COMPLETE` state: Advance to the next level.\n    -   In `GAME_OVER` or `WIN` state: Restart game from Level 1.\nNo special key combinations are used.",
  "game_states": "The game flow is managed through distinct states:\n-   **`MENU` State:**\n    -   **Description:** The initial state. Displays game title, and options like 'Start Game', 'Instructions', 'High Scores'.\n    -   **Transitions:** Pressing `Space` or clicking 'Start Game' button transitions to `PLAYING` state (Level 1).\n-   **`PLAYING` State:**\n    -   **Description:** The active gameplay state. Player controls character, obstacles spawn, score updates, game speed increases.\n    -   **Transitions:**\n        -   Collision with obstacle (without hoverboard) -> `GAME_OVER`.\n        -   `distanceRun` reaches `level.distanceGoal` -> `LEVEL_COMPLETE`.\n        -   Pressing `Space` -> `PAUSED`.\n-   **`PAUSED` State:**\n    -   **Description:** Game logic is suspended. Displays 'Paused' overlay with 'Resume' and 'Quit to Menu' options.\n    -   **Transitions:**\n        -   Pressing `Space` or clicking 'Resume' -> `PLAYING`.\n        -   Clicking 'Quit to Menu' -> `MENU`.\n-   **`LEVEL_COMPLETE` State:**\n    -   **Description:** Game logic suspended. Displays 'Level X Complete!', current score, and a 'Next Level' button.\n    -   **Transitions:**\n        -   Pressing `Space` or clicking 'Next Level': If it's the final level, transitions to `WIN` state; otherwise, transitions to `PLAYING` state for the next level.\n-   **`GAME_OVER` State:**\n    -   **Description:** Game logic suspended. Displays 'Game Over!', final score, distance, and 'Restart' and 'Quit to Menu' buttons. Prompts for high score if achieved.\n    -   **Transitions:**\n        -   Pressing `Space` or clicking 'Restart' -> `PLAYING` (restarts from Level 1).\n        -   Clicking 'Quit to Menu' -> `MENU`.\n-   **`WIN` State:**\n    -   **Description:** Game logic suspended. Displays 'Congratulations! You completed all levels!', final score, distance, and 'Play Again' and 'Quit to Menu' buttons.\n    -   **Transitions:**\n        -   Clicking 'Play Again' -> `PLAYING` (restarts from Level 1).\n        -   Clicking 'Quit to Menu' -> `MENU`.",
  "win_conditions": "The player wins by successfully completing all 5 predefined levels. This occurs when the `distanceRun` (cumulative) reaches the `distanceGoal` for the final (Level 5) level. Upon this event, the game transitions to the `WIN` state.",
  "lose_conditions": "The player loses under the following conditions:\n1.  **Obstacle Collision:** The player's bounding box collides with any obstacle (train, jump barrier, slide barrier) while `player.hoverboardActive` is false. If `player.hoverboardActive` is true, the hoverboard is consumed, and the game continues; otherwise, this leads to `GAME_OVER`.",
  "scoring_system": "A comprehensive scoring system tracks player performance and encourages longer runs:\n-   **Points Awarded:**\n    -   `+10` points for each coin collected.\n    -   `+1` point for every 10 units of distance run (e.g., `distanceRun / 10`).\n    -   `+50` points for collecting any power-up (Jetpack, Hoverboard, Coin Magnet).\n    -   `+500` bonus points awarded upon successfully completing each level.\n-   **Bonus Points/Multipliers:**\n    -   A **Distance Multiplier** is applied to all points earned (coins, distance, power-ups) based on the current level:\n        -   Level 1: `x1` multiplier.\n        -   Level 2: `x1.5` multiplier.\n        -   Level 3: `x2` multiplier.\n        -   Level 4: `x2.5` multiplier.\n        -   Level 5: `x3` multiplier.\n-   **High Score System:** The top 5 highest scores achieved will be stored locally using `localStorage`. This high score list will be accessible from the main `MENU` and displayed on the `GAME_OVER` screen if a new high score is achieved.\n-   **Score Display:**\n    -   **Current Score:** Displayed at the top-left corner of the screen, e.g., `text('SCORE: ' + formattedScore, 10, 30)`.\n    -   **Distance Run:** Displayed at the top-right corner, e.g., `text('DIST: ' + formattedDistance + 'm', width - 150, 30)`.\n    -   **Coins Collected:** Displayed below the score, e.g., `text('COINS: ' + formattedCoins, 10, 50)`.\n    -   All displays should be in a clear, fixed-width font and format (e.g., `SCORE: 000000`, `DIST: 0000m`, `COINS: 000`) for easy readability.\n-   **Score Effect on Gameplay:** The score primarily serves as a metric for player performance and competitive ranking. While high scores don't directly alter current gameplay, they drive player motivation for re-attempts and longer runs across levels.",
  "level_system": "The game, despite its 'endless runner' inspiration, will feature 5 distinct levels, each with increasing difficulty and a specific distance goal. A 'LEVEL: [Current Level]' indicator will be displayed top-center (e.g., `(width / 2, 30)`).\n\n**Level Progression:** Upon reaching the `distanceGoal` for the current level, the game pauses and transitions to the `LEVEL_COMPLETE` state. Players must then choose to proceed to the next level.\n\n**5 Levels:**\n1.  **Level 1: \"Subway Beginner\"**\n    -   **Difficulty:** Easy. Focus on introducing core mechanics.\n    -   **Cumulative Distance Goal:** 2000 units.\n    -   **Initial Game Speed:** 5 units/frame (increases slowly by 0.005 units/frame per second).\n    -   **Obstacles:** Low density (e.g., 20% chance of spawning an obstacle segment). Primarily single-lane trains and jump barriers. Very few slide barriers. Simple patterns.\n    -   **Power-ups:** High spawn frequency (e.g., one power-up spawns every ~500 units of distance).\n2.  **Level 2: \"Urban Explorer\"**\n    -   **Difficulty:** Medium. Introduces more complex obstacle combinations.\n    -   **Cumulative Distance Goal:** 4000 units.\n    -   **Initial Game Speed:** 7 units/frame (increases at 0.008 units/frame per second).\n    -   **Obstacles:** Moderate density (30% chance). More two-lane trains, combinations of jump/slide barriers. Obstacle gaps require quicker decisions.\n    -   **Power-ups:** Reduced spawn frequency (e.g., every ~750 units of distance).\n3.  **Level 3: \"Track Master\"**\n    -   **Difficulty:** Hard. Demands quicker reflexes and pattern recognition.\n    -   **Cumulative Distance Goal:** 7000 units.\n    -   **Initial Game Speed:** 10 units/frame (increases at 0.012 units/frame per second).\n    -   **Obstacles:** High density (40% chance). Frequent multi-lane obstacles, faster successions of jump/slide sequences. Some segments with no clear 'safe' lane.\n    -   **Power-ups:** Further reduced spawn frequency (e.g., every ~1000 units of distance).\n4.  **Level 4: \"Speed Demon\"**\n    -   **Difficulty:** Very Hard. Pushes player to limits with high speed and tight maneuvers.\n    -   **Cumulative Distance Goal:** 11000 units.\n    -   **Initial Game Speed:** 13 units/frame (increases at 0.015 units/frame per second).\n    -   **Obstacles:** Very high density (50% chance). Intense sequences with very little reaction time. Long stretches of multi-lane train walls requiring precise lane changes.\n    -   **Power-ups:** Infrequent spawn (e.g., every ~1250 units of distance).\n5.  **Level 5: \"Endless Legend\"**\n    -   **Difficulty:** Extreme. The ultimate challenge level.\n    -   **Cumulative Distance Goal:** 16000 units.\n    -   **Initial Game Speed:** 16 units/frame (increases at 0.018 units/frame per second).\n    -   **Obstacles:** Maximum density (60% chance). Extremely fast, complex, and unpredictable patterns requiring mastery of all actions. Obstacles designed to force quick decision-making and near-perfect execution.\n    -   **Power-ups:** Very infrequent spawn (e.g., every ~1500 units of distance).\n    -   **Win Condition:** Reaching this level's distance goal triggers the `WIN` state.",
  "simplifications": "To adapt Subway Surfers for a detailed HTML5 p5.js/Matter.js specification and meet specific requirements (finite levels, no multiplayer), the following simplifications are made:\n-   **Character & Hoverboard Unlocking:** All character unlocking, cosmetic customization, and multiple hoverboard types (with unique abilities) are removed. The game features a single default player character and one type of hoverboard power-up. Coin collection is solely for score and not an in-game currency.\n-   **Daily/Weekly Missions:** The concept of daily missions, specific objectives, and rewards (e.g., 'Collect 500 coins', 'Use Jetpack 3 times') is entirely removed to simplify game logic and progression.\n-   **Game Center/Online Leaderboards:** Replaced with a local high score system using `localStorage` to track and display the top 5 scores, removing the complexity of online integration.\n-   **Graphics & Animation Fidelity:** The highly detailed 3D graphics and complex character animations are simplified. Visuals will use p5.js drawing primitives or basic sprites to create a pseudo-3D/2.5D perspective. Player character animations will be basic (e.g., bobbing for running, squashing/stretching for jump/slide). True 3D rendering and advanced lighting/shadows are out of scope.\n-   **Procedural Generation Complexity:** While obstacles are procedurally generated, the generation algorithm will be simplified to combine predefined obstacle 'chunks' or patterns rather than dynamically creating highly adaptive and complex environments.\n-   **Physics Engine (Matter.js):** While Matter.js could be used, the specified physics (jump arc, slide, lane change interpolation) are simple enough to be handled manually with p5.js's drawing and variable manipulation. This avoids the overhead and potential complexity of integrating a full physics engine where rigid body simulation isn't central.\n-   **Sound & Music:** Not included in this specification; assumed to be an enhancement for a later stage of development.",
  "implementation_notes": "1.  **Score Display:** All score-related elements (SCORE, DISTANCE, COINS, LEVEL) will be drawn using `p5.js text()` function. They should have a consistent, legible font, size, and color, and remain visible throughout the `PLAYING` state. In `GAME_OVER`, `LEVEL_COMPLETE`, and `WIN` states, final statistics will be displayed centrally with larger text.\n2.  **Level Progression:** When `distanceRun` meets `level.distanceGoal`, the `LEVEL_COMPLETE` screen appears. The `currentLevel` variable will be incremented, and relevant level parameters (e.g., `gameSpeed`, obstacle spawn rates) will be updated before transitioning back to `PLAYING` for the next level. The `distanceRun` counter is cumulative across levels.\n3.  **Object Pooling:** For performance, obstacles, coins, and power-ups that scroll off-screen (`z` coordinate passes player's position) should not be destroyed and re-created. Instead, they should be marked inactive and reused when new objects need to be spawned, resetting their `z` position and type.\n4.  **Collision Detection:** Use simple AABB (Axis-Aligned Bounding Box) collision checks. The player's bounding box will dynamically adjust its height based on `isJumping` or `isSliding` state. Obstacles will also have predefined bounding boxes.\n5.  **Coordinate System:** A conceptual 3D `(x, y, z)` world will be mapped to a 2D canvas. `x` will represent lane position, `y` will represent vertical position (jump/slide), and `z` will represent depth. Objects will move along the `z` axis towards `z=0` (player's fixed 'camera' position) and scale their size and `y` position for a perspective effect (larger and lower on screen when closer).\n6.  **Game Loop:** The core game loop will involve updating player state, moving all active world objects based on `gameSpeed`, spawning new objects, performing collision checks, updating score/distance, and then rendering all elements."
}
</task>
<example_html>
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8" />
    <style>
      html, body { margin: 0; padding: 0; height: 100%; overflow: hidden; background: #222; }
      body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center; align-items: center; }
      canvas { border: 1px solid #333; width: 600px !important; height: 400px !important; }
      .control-buttons { display: flex; gap: 10px; margin-bottom: 10px; flex-wrap: wrap; justify-content: center; }
      .control-button { padding: 8px 16px; cursor: pointer; background: #444; color: #fff; border: none; border-radius: 4px; }
      .control-button.active { background: #007bff; }
    </style>
  </head>
  <body>
    <div style="text-align: center; margin-bottom: 20px;">
      <h1 id="gameTitle" style="color: #fff; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; margin-bottom: 10px;">{game_title}</h1>
      <div class="control-buttons">
        <button id="humanModeBtn" class="control-button active" onclick="window.setControlMode('HUMAN')">Human Mode</button>
        <button id="test_1_ModeBtn" class="control-button" onclick="window.setControlMode('TEST_1')">TEST (Basic testing)</button>
        <button id="test_2_ModeBtn" class="control-button" onclick="window.setControlMode('TEST_2')">TEST (Win)</button>
      </div>
      <p id="gameDescription" style="color: #ccc; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto 20px auto; line-height: 1.4;">{game_description}</p>

Subway Surfers

Navigate through an endless subway tunnel across three parallel tracks! Dodge trains, jump over barriers, slide under obstacles, and collect coins while running at increasing speeds. Complete all 5 challenging levels by reaching distance goals. Collect power-ups like Jetpacks, Hoverboards, and Coin Magnets to help you survive longer. Your score increases with distance, coins, and level completion bonuses!

Arrow Left/A: Move to left lane | Arrow Right/D: Move to right lane | Arrow Up/W: Jump over low obstacles | Arrow Down/S: Slide under high obstacles | Enter: Start game (from start screen) | ESC: Pause/Unpause game | R: Restart (return to start screen)
